1	---
2	title: Features
3	description: "DDx platform capabilities — stable, beta, and planned."
4	---
5	
6	DDx provides a focused set of platform primitives for AI-assisted development. Each capability is listed with its current maturity level.
7	
8	## Core Platform
9	
10	### Bead Tracker {{< maturity "stable" >}}
11	
12	Beads are the atomic unit of work in DDx. Each bead carries a title, description, acceptance criteria, dependency graph, and full state history. The `ddx bead` subcommands cover the complete CRUD lifecycle.
13	
14	```
15	ddx bead create "Add login endpoint" --ac "returns 200 on valid credentials"
16	ddx bead list --status ready
17	ddx bead close <id>
18	```
19	
20	### Agent Dispatch {{< maturity "stable" >}}
21	
22	Invoke AI agents through a single interface. DDx selects the configured harness, passes the prompt and context, and records token usage and cost per run.
23	
24	```
25	ddx agent run --harness claude --prompt prompts/implement.md
26	ddx agent execute-bead <id>
27	```
28	
29	### Execution Engine {{< maturity "stable" >}}
30	
31	Each bead executes in an isolated git worktree. Evidence is captured after every run. Passing beads are merged back; timed-out or failing runs are preserved for inspection.
32	
33	```
34	ddx agent execute-loop          # drain the queue
35	ddx agent execute-bead <id> --from HEAD
36	```
37	
38	### Plugin System {{< maturity "stable" >}}
39	
40	Plugins package additional resources — templates, prompts, patterns, personas, workflows. One command installs a plugin under the project root — no global state, no machine-wide side effects.
41	
42	```
43	ddx install <plugin-name>       # install a plugin
44	ddx install ddx                 # update DDx default resources
45	```
46	
47	### Document Library {{< maturity "stable" >}}
48	
49	Prompts, patterns, templates, and personas live in versioned library directories. The library ships with DDx and is extended by plugins.
50	
51	```
52	ddx prompts list
53	ddx templates apply scaffold
54	ddx persona list
55	```
56	
57	### Artifact Graph {{< maturity "stable" >}}
58	
59	DDx tracks the relationships between project artifacts — both markdown documents and non-markdown files. Each non-markdown artifact carries a `.ddx.yaml` sidecar that records its kind, provenance, and links to other artifacts, so the graph stays consistent across formats.
60	
61	```
62	ddx doc graph
63	ddx doc audit
64	```
65	
66	### Git Sync {{< maturity "stable" >}}
67	
68	DDx resources are versioned via git subtree. `ddx update` pulls the latest library from the master repository; `ddx contribute` pushes improvements back.
69	
70	```
71	ddx update
72	ddx contribute
73	```
74	
75	## Extended Capabilities
76	
77	### MCP Server {{< maturity "beta" >}}
78	
79	DDx can expose beads, documents, and execution history over the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and HTTP. Remote supervisors — including Claude Desktop and compatible clients — can query work state and steer execution.
80	
81	```
82	ddx server start
83	```
84	
85	### Web UI {{< maturity "beta" >}}
86	
87	A browser interface for the bead tracker and document library. Built with SvelteKit and embedded in the `ddx-server` binary. Useful for reviewing bead state, execution history, and document relationships without leaving the browser.
88	
89	### Cost-Tiered Routing {{< maturity "beta" >}}
90	
91	Configure multiple model endpoints with tier labels. DDx routes beads to the cheapest model that meets the complexity threshold, escalating on failure. Optimize closed-work-per-dollar across Claude, Codex, Gemini, and local models.
92	
93	## Planned
94	
95	### Remote Execution {{< maturity "planned" >}}
96	
97	Run beads on remote compute — a cloud VM, a CI runner, or a dedicated agent host — without changing the local workflow. The bead queue and merge result flow stay identical.
98	
99	### Team Sync {{< maturity "planned" >}}
100	
101	Shared bead queues across a team. Agents on multiple machines claim, execute, and close beads from the same queue without coordination overhead.
102

1	---
2	title: Principles
3	weight: 2
4	---
5	
6	DDx is shaped by a small set of load-bearing principles. They drive the
7	trade-offs you see across the CLI, the bead tracker, and the agent service.
8	Each principle here is one we cite in real design and review decisions — if it
9	weren't, it wouldn't be on the page.
10	
11	## Platform, Not Methodology
12	
13	DDx provides primitives. Workflow tools (HELIX) and quality tools (Dun)
14	provide opinions. The CLI owns the document library, bead tracker, agent
15	dispatch, personas, templates, and git sync. Phases, gates, supervisory loops,
16	and methodology validation belong in plugins.
17	
18	**In practice:** when someone proposes adding a "frame phase" command or a
19	"requirements gate" check to `ddx`, the answer is no — those live in the HELIX
20	plugin. When two workflow plugins need the same primitive (say, content
21	hashing), it gets lifted into DDx.
22	
23	## Project-Local by Default
24	
25	`ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only write under `<projectRoot>`. There
26	is no home-directory state and no machine-wide opinions. The single global
27	artifact is `ddx-server`. Per-user concerns (API keys, model preferences) are
28	the user's responsibility, not DDx's install surface.
29	
30	**In practice:** a project's plugin tree lives at `<projectRoot>/.ddx/plugins/`,
31	its skills at `<projectRoot>/.claude/skills/`, its bead store inside the repo.
32	Cloning the repo gives you the whole picture; nothing on the developer's
33	laptop changes the answer.
34	
35	## Documents Are the Product
36	
37	Code is output. Documents are what you maintain. Prompts, personas, patterns,
38	templates, and specs are first-class, versioned, agent-consumable artifacts —
39	not afterthoughts you write once and forget.
40	
41	**In practice:** when a spec changes, beads are created for the work the
42	change implies. When implementation reveals the spec was wrong, the spec is
43	updated and the implementation follows. The artifact hierarchy is a set of
44	lenses, not a one-way pipeline.
45	
46	## Beads Are the Unit of Work
47	
48	Work flows through beads — self-contained items with acceptance criteria and
49	a dependency DAG. The queue is the interface between intent and execution.
50	Anything that needs to happen should be expressible as a bead; anything an
51	agent does should be against a bead.
52	
53	**In practice:** a developer noticing a bug doesn't fix it inline — they
54	`ddx bead create`, link dependencies, and either pick it up or let
55	`ddx work` drain the queue. The bead is the contract; review checks the
56	acceptance criteria, not the developer's memory of the conversation.
57	
58	## Cost-Tiered Throughput
59	
60	Optimize closed beads per dollar, not raw capability. Cheap models do; strong
61	models review; deterministic checks (Dun) sit at the top of the ladder
62	catching what review missed. Routing is by capability and endpoint, not by
63	hardcoded provider name.
64	
65	**In practice:** the execute-loop runs implementation on a cheap model in an
66	isolated worktree, then routes the diff to a stronger reviewer. Failed
67	reviews escalate to a higher tier on the next attempt. The agent service
68	discovers models from endpoints rather than wiring named providers.
69	
70	## Git-Native, File-First
71	
72	Plain files. Standard git. No lock-in. Documents live in the repo, beads live
73	in the repo, plugins live in the repo. Sync is `git pull` and `git push` —
74	not a proprietary protocol.
75	
76	**In practice:** beads are JSONL on disk; you can read them with `cat`,
77	diff them, and merge them. Library documents are Markdown and YAML; you can
78	edit them in any tool. There is no DDx server you must connect to.
79	
80	## Agent-Agnostic
81	
82	Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out contract plugs into the agent
83	service. Claude, Codex, Gemini, local models — all use the same prompt
84	envelope, the same session logging, the same quorum review.
85	
86	**In practice:** `ddx agent run --harness claude` and `ddx agent run --harness
87	qwen-local` differ in cost and capability, not in interface. Routing decisions
88	are observable, swappable, and don't require touching the CLI.
89	
90	## Make Intent Explicit
91	
92	Code, configs, and documents should say what they mean. Avoid implicit
93	conventions, magic values, and behavior that depends on undocumented ordering.
94	When intent is ambiguous, name it.
95	
96	**In practice:** persona bindings are explicit in `.ddx.yml`, not inferred
97	from filenames. Bead acceptance criteria are checked items, not prose
98	expectations. A reviewer can tell what was intended without asking.
99	
100	## Prefer Reversible Decisions
101	
102	When two options are otherwise equivalent, choose the one easier to undo.
103	Commit to irreversible choices deliberately, with documented rationale.
104	
105	**In practice:** endpoint-based agent routing is preferred over hardcoded
106	provider names because endpoints are swappable. Project-local install is
107	preferred over global state because it's reversible by deleting `.ddx/`.
108	
109	## When Principles Conflict
110	
111	Apply the one whose violation would cause the worse outcome in the current
112	context. Document the tension in the commit message or design note. Common
113	tensions:
114	
115	- **Platform vs. Simplicity** — keeping plugins out of the CLI sometimes
116	  costs duplication across plugins. Accept the duplication until two plugins
117	  prove the primitive is shared infrastructure.
118	- **Cost-Tiered vs. Reversible Decisions** — routing logic that locks in a
119	  vendor is cheap to ship and expensive to undo. Prefer endpoint abstractions
120	  even when a single provider would be simpler today.
121	
122	For the canonical version of these principles (terse, contributor-facing),
123	see `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md` in the repository.
124

3	  id: ddx-concerns
4	  depends_on:
5	    - helix.prd
6	---
7	# Project Concerns
8	
9	## Active Concerns
10	
11	- go-std (tech-stack, cli/api)
12	- typescript-bun (tech-stack, ui)
13	- e2e-playwright (testing, ui/site)
14	- frontend-design (design-system, ui)
15	- hugo-hextra (microsite, site)
16	- demo-asciinema (demo, all)
17	- security-owasp (security, all)
18	- testing (quality-attribute, all)
19	
20	## Area Labels
21	
22	| Label | Applies to |
23	|-------|-----------|
24	| `all` | Every bead |
25	| `cli` | Core DDx binary, commands, internal packages |
26	| `api` | Server HTTP/MCP endpoints |
27	| `agent` | DDx agent runtime, execution, routing, and migration work |
28	| `bead` | Bead tracker maintenance, bead-governance reviews, and bead-scoped workflow contract work |
29	| `ui` | Web UI frontend (SvelteKit embedded in Go binary via embed.FS) |
30	| `site` | website/, microsite content and deployment |
31	| `docs` | Governing docs, plans, reviews, and alignment reports |
32	| `infra` | CI, Docker, release infrastructure |
33	
34	Bead-scoped work uses `area:bead` as an additive label, not a replacement for any domain-specific area labels. A bead should still carry the broader labels that match the work it governs so concern matching remains explicit.
35	
36	## Project Overrides
37	
38	### go-std
39	
40	- **Source**: Go source is in `cli/` within this monorepo
41	- **CLI framework**: Cobra with CommandFactory pattern
42	- **Testing**: `go test` with acceptance, integration, contract, and E2E smoke tests
43	- **Linter**: golangci-lint v2 with project `.golangci.yml`
44	- **Build**: `make build` in `cli/`, multi-platform via `make build-all`
45	- **Pre-commit**: lefthook runs `go fmt`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint`, `go test`
46	
47	### typescript-bun
48	
49	- **Build tool**: SvelteKit (not Bun's native bundler) per ADR-002. SvelteKit provides
50	  hot module replacement and the plugin ecosystem for the embedded SPA.
51	- **Test runner**: Playwright for E2E (not `bun:test`). Tests need the Go
52	  server running.
53	- **Formatter**: Project uses existing SvelteKit/TypeScript conventions
54	  (prettier-plugin-svelte + eslint-plugin-svelte). Biome migration deferred.
55	- **Package manager**: Bun for install and scripts (`bun install`, `bun run build`).
56	- **Lockfile**: `bun.lock` committed (not `package-lock.json`).
57	- **Runtime**: Frontend is browser-only (SvelteKit embedded in Go binary via
58	  `embed.FS`). Bun-native server APIs (`Bun.serve()`) do not apply.
59	- **Data layer**: GraphQL with graphql-request + graphql-ws for typed queries and real-time subscriptions per ADR-002 v2.
60	- **Location**: `cli/internal/server/frontend/`
61	
62	### e2e-playwright
63	
64	Three Playwright configurations, each deliberately scoped to a
65	different backend so tests stay decoupled:
66	
67	1. **Frontend functional + visual regression** (`cli/internal/server/frontend/playwright.config.ts`):
68	   - Tests in `cli/internal/server/frontend/e2e/`
69	   - Screenshots in `e2e/*.spec.ts-snapshots/`
70	   - Runs against the **SvelteKit static preview** on port 4173
71	     (`bun run build && bun run preview`). The preview serves the same
72	     static bundle embedded into the Go binary; GraphQL is mocked at
73	     the request layer in each spec.
74	   - Rationale: functional + visual tests should not depend on a live
75	     Go server or real GraphQL data. Request-route interception keeps
76	     snapshots deterministic.
77	
78	2. **Demo recording** (`cli/internal/server/frontend/playwright.demo.config.ts`):
79	   - `demo-recording.spec.ts` navigates all 5 pages with interactions
80	   - Runs against the **Go server** on port 18080 (real binary, real
81	     data) to produce representative videos.
82	   - Video recording at 1280x720
83	   - Output in `demo-output/`
84	   - Run via `bun run demo:record`
85	
86	3. **Microsite** (`website/playwright.config.ts`):
87	   - Tests in `website/e2e/microsite.spec.ts`
88	   - Screenshots for homepage, docs, navigation
89	   - Runs against `hugo server` on the configured port
90	
91	### frontend-design
92	
93	Cross-cutting design and accessibility concern for every UI surface DDx ships
94	(the SvelteKit web UI in `cli/internal/server/frontend/` and any future
95	embedded views). Applies to every feature that touches the UI — individual
96	feature specs do not restate its rules.
97	
98	**Authoritative guide for aesthetic/UX judgement**
99	
100	The installed `anthropics/skills@frontend-design` skill is the authoritative
101	guide for creating and reviewing UI surfaces under this concern. When a
102	feature requires a design judgement (layout, typography, interaction
103	affordance, visual hierarchy), delegate the judgement to that skill; the
104	concern below captures the non-negotiable constraints the skill's output
105	must satisfy.
106	
107	**Palette discipline**
108	
109	- Colors reference semantic tokens (e.g. `status.open`, `status.blocked`,
110	  `priority.p0`, `tier.cheap`) centralized in Tailwind config and/or
111	  `$lib/stores/theme.ts`. Raw hex in component markup is a drift signal;
112	  CI greps for `#[0-9a-fA-F]{3,8}` outside the theme files and fails.
113	- Every colored state has an accompanying non-color cue (text label, icon,
114	  pattern). Status conveyed by color alone is a violation.
115	- Status/priority/tier vocabularies use the SAME token across all pages so
116	  a "blocked" bead and a "blocked" worker look identical.
117	
118	**Dark / light parity**
119	
120	- Every page is legible in both dark and light modes without hover, focus,
121	  or menu interaction. The top-nav theme toggle switches modes; choice
122	  persists to localStorage and is bookmarkable via `?theme=dark|light`.
123	- Visual regression: `screenshots.spec.ts` captures EACH page twice —
124	  once per theme. Threshold-gated diffs catch palette drift.
125	
126	**Accessibility floor**
127	
128	- WCAG AA: 4.5:1 contrast for normal text, 3:1 for large text, in both
129	  modes.
130	- Every interactive control reachable by keyboard; focus rings visible
131	  in both themes.
132	- `@axe-core/playwright` runs against every page; CI fails on any
133	  `critical` or `serious` violation. Lane: `bun run test:a11y`.
134	
135	**Quality gates (CI-enforced)**
136	
137	| Gate | Command | Frequency |
138	|------|---------|-----------|
139	| Palette discipline grep | pre-commit lefthook check on `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/**/*.{svelte,ts}` | per commit |
140	| Axe-core page scans | `bun run test:a11y` | CI + pre-push |
141	| Dark/light visual regression | `playwright screenshots.spec.ts` with theme matrix | CI |
142	| Keyboard-reachability smoke | Playwright `tab` through interactive controls on each page | CI |
143	
144	**Drift signals (alignment-review fodder)**
145	
146	- Raw hex color in a component (grep miss) → palette discipline broken.
147	- Page visible only in one theme (manual reviewer's report; CI catches
148	  via visual-regression diff) → dark/light parity broken.
149	- A11y scan surfaces a new `critical` violation → blocking; not a warning.
150	- A user story's acceptance criterion restates "must work in dark mode"
151	  or "must be WCAG-AA" → the concern is leaking into feature specs;
152	  remove the restatement.
153	
154	### hugo-hextra
155	
156	- **Theme version**: Hextra pinned in `website/go.mod`
157	- **Deployment**: GitHub Pages at `DocumentDrivenDX.github.io/ddx/`
158	- **Custom shortcode**: `asciinema.html` for terminal recording embeds
159	- **E2E tests**: Playwright tests in `website/e2e/`
160	
161	### demo-asciinema
162	
163	- **Recording environment**: Docker containers for reproducibility. All
164	  recording tools (asciinema, agg) are containerized.
165	- **Demo scripts**: `scripts/demos/` with deterministic shell scripts
166	- **Embedding**: asciinema shortcode loads player from CDN, plays `.cast`
167	  files from `website/static/demos/`
168	- **Agent credentials**: Demos invoking AI agents mount `~/.claude.json` (ro)
169	  and `~/.claude/` (rw) from host into container.
170	
171	### security-owasp
172	
173	- **Dependency audit**: `govulncheck ./...` for Go, `bun audit` for frontend
174	- **Secret scanning**: lefthook pre-commit hook via gitleaks
175	- **Path traversal**: Server validates all document paths, rejects `..`
176	- **Localhost guard**: All write and dispatch endpoints reject non-localhost requests
177	- **Network auth**: ts-net (Tailscale) for authenticated remote access per ADR-006.
178	  No custom API keys. Identity from tailnet membership.
179	
180	### testing
181	
182	Inherits the full HELIX `testing` concern (see `workflows/concerns/testing/concern.md`). The rules below are DDx-specific applications of those principles, not replacements.
183	
184	**Tooling**
185	
186	- **Go tests**: Acceptance, integration, contract, E2E smoke in `cli/cmd/`
187	  and `cli/internal/*/`
188	- **Frontend tests**: Playwright E2E against running server
189	- **Pre-commit**: lefthook runs full Go test suite on changed packages
190	- **CI**: GitHub Actions runs `lefthook run ci` + E2E smoke tests
191	
192	**No mocks, period — applied to DDx**
193	
194	The HELIX concern says: *"Use real implementations where practical… Mock only at true system boundaries… Never mock the thing you are testing."* In DDx that becomes an absolute rule:
195	
196	**Integration tests: zero mocks.** No exceptions. Integration tests exercise real production implementations of every dependency in the graph — real git (`t.TempDir()` + scrubbed `GIT_*` env), real filesystem, real bead store, real coordinator, real server, real agent harness (the `script` harness below is the only approved fake for the AI-provider boundary, and it does not mock — it performs real filesystem and git operations deterministically). A test that substitutes any mock for a production component is not an integration test. It is a unit test that is lying about its coverage.
197	
198	**Unit tests: stubs, not mocks, and only at your own domain interfaces.** When a unit test needs to isolate the code under test from a dependency, the approved pattern is:
199	
200	1. Define the dependency as an interface (e.g., `bead.Store`, `agent.ExecuteBeadLoopStore`, `agent.LandingGitOps`).
201	2. Write an **in-memory stub** that implements the interface — not a call-recording mock.
202	3. The stub must pass the SAME contract test suite as the production implementation. When both implementations pass the same tests, they are proven interchangeable. This is the "contract test for the stub" pattern: you write one suite of tests against the interface, and run it against both the in-memory stub and the real backend. Either both pass or both fail.
203	4. Unit tests wire the code under test with the in-memory stub, not with a mock. Assertions are on behavior (did the right bead close?), not call sequence (was `Close` called exactly once with these args?).
204	
205	`testify/mock` is not used in new tests. `gomock` is not used. Call-sequence assertions are not used. If a test relies on asserting that method X was called Y times with argument Z, it is testing implementation details, not behavior — delete or rewrite.
206	
207	**Never mock the thing you are testing.** A test of the land coordinator that substitutes a fake `LandingGitOps` is mocking the thing under test. Coordinator tests use `RealLandingGitOps` against a real git repo. Same for execute-bead, gate evaluation, merge logic, preserve-ref writes, and every other orchestration concern.
208	
209	**The only approved fake in the DDx test suite is the `script` harness.** It is the *AI-provider* boundary replacement, and it is not a mock — it is a deterministic alternate implementation of the `agent.Runner` interface that reads a line-based directive file and performs real filesystem + git operations against the worktree. The `virtual` harness is a replay-only fake retained for prompt-routing unit tests; it does not write files or make commits.
210	
211	**Contract-test pattern for new interfaces.** When you introduce a new domain interface with an in-memory stub:
212	
213	```go
214	// contract_test.go — runs the same suite against both implementations
215	func TestStoreContract(t *testing.T) {
216	    cases := []struct{ name string; build func(t *testing.T) Store }{
217	        {"memory", func(t *testing.T) Store { return NewMemoryStore() }},
218	        {"file",   func(t *testing.T) Store { return NewFileStore(t.TempDir()) }},
219	    }
220	    for _, c := range cases {
221	        t.Run(c.name, func(t *testing.T) {
222	            s := c.build(t)
223	            // one set of test cases, run against both implementations
224	            t.Run("CreateAndGet", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
225	            t.Run("UpdateLosesNoFields", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
226	            t.Run("ConcurrentWritersSerialized", func(t *testing.T) { /* ... */ })
227	        })
228	    }
229	}
230	```
231	
232	If the stub passes the contract suite but the real backend fails, either the backend is broken or the stub is lying. Either way the contract suite exposes the drift immediately.
233	
234	**DDx-specific drift signals (extend HELIX's list)**
235	
236	- `orchTestGitOps`, `fakeExecuteBeadGit`, `gateTestGitOps`, or any struct that implements a git-shaped interface for tests → replace with real git in temp repos. Existing instances are legacy and must be migrated as the tests around them are touched.
237	- `TestConcurrentWorkers*` tests that instantiate one mock git per goroutine → the shared-writer invariant is not being tested. Replace with real git + the `script` harness.
238	- `testify/mock` or `gomock` for anything other than third-party SDK boundaries → use stubs or real implementations.
239	- Unit tests that claim to cover "merge" or "rebase" or "land" logic without running `git log` or `git rev-list` → broken test.
240	- `--compare` / comparison harness tests using `mockExecutor` without a real temp repo → legacy from FEAT-019, flag for migration.
241	
242	**Canonical fakes and helpers**
243	
244	- `cli/internal/agent/script.go` — deterministic `script` harness (the fake AI agent)
245	- `cli/internal/agent/integration_helper_test.go` — `newScriptHarnessRepo(t, beadCount)` helper for integration tests
246	- `cli/internal/git/git_basic_test.go` — `runGitInDir` helper for scrubbed-env git subprocesses
247	- `cli/internal/bead/store_test.go` — pattern for real bead store in temp dir
248	
249	## ADR References
250	
251	| Concern | ADR | Decision |
252	|---------|-----|----------|
253	| typescript-bun | ADR-002 | Web stack: Bun + SvelteKit + Tailwind |
254	| typescript-bun | ADR-005 | GraphQL + graphql-request client-side data layer for beads UI |
255	| security-owasp | ADR-003 | Package integrity via commit SHA + tree hash |
256	| security-owasp | ADR-004 | Bead-backed collections for runtime storage |
257	| security-owasp | ADR-006 | ts-net for network authentication (no API keys) |

8	
9	**Version:** 4.2.0
10	**Date:** 2026-04-29
11	**Status:** Active
12	
13	## Summary
14	
15	DDx is a monorepo producing three artifacts that together form the shared
16	local-first infrastructure for document-driven development:
17	
18	1. **`ddx` CLI** — multi-media artifact library management, artifact graph
19	   operations, bead tracking, three-layer run architecture (`ddx run` /
20	   `ddx try` / `ddx work`) on a unified on-disk substrate, agent dispatch,
21	   persona composition, template application, and git sync
22	2. **`ddx-server`** — web server + MCP endpoints for browsing documents,
23	   artifacts, beads, agent session logs, and execution history over the network
24	3. **`ddx.github.io`** — promotional website explaining DDx to developers and
25	   linking to docs
26	
27	DDx is the foundation layer. Workflow tools (HELIX, others) build on top. DDx
28	provides reusable local services; it does not impose workflow phases or
29	methodology.
30

38	  and `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` namespaces for cross-layer
39	  evidence introspection
40	- FEAT-002 defines the server, HTTP, and MCP surfaces
41	- FEAT-003 defines the promotional website and documentation
42	- FEAT-004 defines shared work-item storage
43	- FEAT-005 defines the artifact convention and sidecar schema: identity
44	  broadens to non-markdown via sidecar `.ddx.yaml`; `media_type` and

46	  artifact; authority rule: identity present → artifact
47	- FEAT-006 defines the layer-1 agent-dispatch boundary: the `ddx run`

49	  `ddx agent` mounts the upstream agent Cobra root structurally; non-bead
50	  profile and permissions selection; session-log envelope owned by DDx, inner
51	  log shape by upstream
52	- FEAT-007 defines the artifact graph and staleness model: sidecar-aware
53	  scanner; `media_type` field; `generated_by` edge with a separate provenance
54	  staleness rule (does not cascade like `depends_on`); 100% read endpoints for
55	  graph state, generated-artifact metadata, and sidecar content
56	- FEAT-008 defines the embedded web UI: media-type-aware rendering (markdown,
57	  mermaid SVG, excalidraw embed, image inline, PDF embed); regenerate action
58	  wired to `artifactRegenerate`; layer-aware run views (`work` → `try` → `run`
59	  drill-down)
60	- FEAT-009 defines the online library and plugin registry
61	- FEAT-010 defines the three-layer run architecture and unified substrate:
62	  `ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` as explicit primitives; one on-disk
63	  record shape with layer metadata; `.ddx/exec-runs/` and
64	  `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` collapse into one layout; `ddx work`
65	  no-progress detection and stop conditions; `artifactRegenerate` as the only
66	  write surface added in this plan
67	- FEAT-011 defines agent-facing skills for DDx CLI operations
68	- FEAT-012 defines git awareness: auto-commit for documents and bead tracker,
69	  document history, write-then-commit for MCP/UI clients, and agent guidance
70	  generation on init
71	- FEAT-013 defines multi-agent coordination: concurrent bead safety,
72	  MCP supervisor surface, worktree-aware dispatch
73	- FEAT-014 defines agent token awareness: usage tracking, budget enforcement,
74	  and model selection guidance across harnesses
75	- FEAT-015 defines the installation architecture: clean separation of
76	  install.sh (binary), ddx install --global (skills), ddx init (project),
77	  and ddx install <plugin> (plugin lifecycle)
78	- FEAT-016 defines process metrics: bead lifecycle cost, rework rates, and
79	  derived measures computed from existing stores (beads, agent sessions).
80	  Distinct from FEAT-010 which covers operational metrics you *run*.
81	- ~~FEAT-017~~: adversarial review is a form of multi-agent dispatch covered by
82	  FEAT-006 quorum infrastructure. The "review against governing artifacts →
83	  structured findings → beads" workflow needs a design cycle to find the right
84	  abstraction, not a standalone feature.
85	- FEAT-018 defines plugin API documentation and stability: document existing
86	  extension surfaces (package.yaml, plugin directory layout, SKILL.md, hooks,
87	  bead conventions), add schema versioning, commit to backward compatibility
88	- FEAT-019 defines evaluation UX as a child of FEAT-010: comparison views,
89	  grading rubric storage and display, and benchmark result aggregation.
90	  Workflow shapes (comparison dispatch, replay, benchmark) move to the skills
91	  library — FEAT-019 does not own run orchestration
92	- FEAT-020 defines server node state and project registry: the server acquires
93	  a stable node identity (hostname or DDX_NODE_NAME), persists a project
94	  registry in an XDG-standard JSON file, writes a discovery addr file, and

98	  with node/project-aware routing so every view is bookmarkable

5	# Product Vision: DDx
6	
7	**Version:** 2.1.0
8	**Date:** 2026-04-29
9	**Status:** Active
10	
11	## Core Thesis
12	
13	> Software is leverage. Fifty years of practice has shown that there are physics of software that must be respected to produce and maintain systems at scale. The agentic era allows every team to produce systems at scale and makes this even more important.
14	>
15	> Generative AI brings its own physics. DDx exists at the seam where these two meet — the fulcrum that lets software's lever do work. Without it, shipping software with agents quietly degrades into prompt-and-pray. The six principles below are the load-bearing claims on both sides.
16	
17	### Physics of Software
18	
19	1. **Abstraction is the lever.** Multi-level artifact stacks (vision → spec → test → code) with maintained relationships are how intent propagates without being lost. True for human teams; load-bearing for agents because they don't carry tacit knowledge between invocations.
20	2. **Software is iteration over tracked work.** Repeated trials over an explicit work substrate — beads, queues, dependency DAGs — is how software gets built. Pre-existed agents; agents make the substrate non-optional.
21	3. **Methodology is plural.** Different teams, projects, and problem domains demand different workflows — waterfall, agile, kanban, HELIX, ad-hoc. No tool that bakes one in survives the rest. DDx provides primitives (artifacts, runs, beads) that any methodology composes.
22	
23	### Physics of Generative AI
24	
25	4. **LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly.** Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and 'cheapest model that works' are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations.
26	5. **Evidence provides memory.** Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what we captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.
27	
28	### The Intersection — DDx's Reason to Exist
29	
30	6. **Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum.** Abstraction levers intent across the artifact stack, but only collaboration converts leverage into shipped software. Humans supply intent and accountability; AI supplies volume and execution. DDx is the toolkit at the seam — handoffs in both directions, at every level.
31	
32	## The Three-Layer Stack
33	
34	DDx is one layer in a three-project stack with explicit boundaries:
35	
36	| Layer | Project | Owns |
37	|-------|---------|------|
38	| Platform | **DDx** (this repo) | Document library, bead tracker, agent dispatch, personas, templates, git sync |
39	| Workflow | **HELIX** | Phases, gates, supervisory dispatch, bounded actions, methodology |
40	| Quality | **Dun** | Check discovery, execution, agent-friendly output |
41	
42	DDx provides primitives. HELIX and others provide opinions. Dun verifies the
43	result. Each layer is independently useful and replaceable.
44	
45	## Mission
46	
47	Make artifacts the unit of software development. Provide the shared
48	infrastructure that developers and workflow tools use to maintain, compose, and
49	deliver the artifacts agents produce and consume to build software.
50	
51	## What DDx Provides
52	
53	- **Artifact library** — structured, versioned, agent-discoverable artifacts in
54	  the repository. Documents, diagrams, wireframes, prompts, images, and other
55	  media; generators checked into git with source-hash-driven regeneration.
56	  Personas, patterns, templates, MCP server registry. Artifacts may be produced
57	  or consumed by humans and agents alike; DDx manages identity and provenance
58	  across all four combinations.
59	- **Multi-media artifact graph** — artifact identity, relationships, and content
60	  hashing across document and non-document media. Any file with a sidecar
61	  `.ddx.yaml` is a first-class artifact.
62	- **Bead tracker** — work items with a dependency DAG, ready/blocked queues,
63	  and JSONL interchange. Beads are the unit of work agents execute.
64	- **Three-layer run architecture** — `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx
65	  try` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). Agent
66	  invocation is upstream; DDx owns worktree lifecycle, evidence bundling, and
67	  drain.
68	- **Project-local install** — `ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only touch
69	  `<projectRoot>`. The only global artifact is `ddx-server`. No home-directory
70	  state, no machine-wide opinions.
71	- **Single `ddx` skill** — DDx ships one consolidated skill, not a fleet. One
72	  surface for agents to learn.
73	
74	## What DDx Is Not
75	
76	- **Not a methodology.** No phases, no gates, no prescribed artifact types.
77	  Workflow tools (HELIX) own those.
78	- **Not a storage system.** Files in Git. No proprietary backend.
79	- **Not an editor or IDE.** Editing happens wherever the user works.
80	- **Not opinionated about agents.** Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out
81	  contract plugs in.
82	
83	## Key Differentiators
84	
85	### vs. Ad-Hoc Agentic Coding
86	
87	Code-as-system-of-record produces point changes without coupling awareness, no
88	structured intent, and constant context re-explanation. DDx makes documents the
89	system of record for intent and architecture; code remains the system of record
90	for implementation.
91	
92	### vs. Code-Only Agent Tools
93	
94	Without an abstraction hierarchy, functionality accumulates in non-orthogonal
95	interfaces and agents cannot reason about cross-cutting impact. DDx gives every
96	change a defined place and a known impact boundary.
97	
98	### vs. Traditional Documentation-Driven Development
99	
100	Prior DDD failed because documents went stale. DDx counters that with
101	relationships, content hashing, beads as reconciliation tasks, and adversarial
102	review.
103	
104	## Design Philosophy
105	
106	### Platform vs Workflow Boundary
107	
108	The hardest discipline is keeping platform code free of methodology opinions.
109	The boundary is concrete:
110	
111	| DDx (Platform) | Workflow Tool (e.g., HELIX) |
112	|----------------|------------------------------|
113	| Document storage, versioning, sync | Artifact types and templates |
114	| Bead CRUD, dependency DAG, ready queue | Phase definitions, gates, ratchets |
115	| Agent dispatch, quorum, session logging | Methodology prompts, supervisory loops |
116	| Persona binding mechanism | Which personas fulfill which roles |
117	| Execute-loop runtime | When to run it, what to do with results |
118	
119	If a feature request would push opinions into the CLI, it belongs in a plugin.
120	
121	### Multi-Directional Iteration
122	
123	The artifact hierarchy is a set of lenses, not a pipeline. Vision changes
124	propagate down; implementation discoveries feed up; spec refinements ripple
125	both ways. DDx's primitives (beads, document relationships, content hashing)
126	support all three directions equally.
127	
128	### Cost-Tiered Execution
129	
130	Closed-bead throughput per dollar is the optimization target. Cheap models do
131	the work; stronger models review; deterministic checks (Dun) sit at the top of
132	the ladder catching what review missed. The agent service routes by capability,
133	not by name — endpoints with live model discovery, not hardcoded providers.
134	
135	### Three-Layer Run Architecture
136	
137	DDx owns three explicit layered primitives:
138	
139	| Layer | CLI | Owns |
140	|-------|-----|------|
141	| 1 | `ddx run` | Single agent invocation; structured output, tokens, model, exit metadata |
142	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | Bead attempt in isolated worktree; merge or preserve result |
143	| 3 | `ddx work` | Mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection and stop conditions |
144	
145	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
146	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records. Agent invocation is upstream
147	(DDx-agent module via CONTRACT-003); DDx owns worktree lifecycle, evidence
148	bundling, and drain loop.
149	
150	### Human-Agent Control Slider
151	
152	DDx supports a continuum from full autonomy (drain the queue overnight) to
153	human-driven (agent assists, human commits). The same primitives serve both;
154	the workflow layer chooses where to sit.
155	
156	## Strategic Fit
157	
158	**Why us:** DDx grew out of daily agent-driven development. The problems we
159	solve are the problems we hit shipping software with agents.
160	
161	**Why now:** Document quality is the bottleneck. Every team is independently
162	rebuilding the same document infrastructure. No shared layer exists.
163	
164	**North Star:** Every developer working with AI agents uses DDx, or tools built
165	on DDx, to manage the documents that drive those agents.
166	
167	## Principles
168	
169	See `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md`. The load-bearing ones:
170	
171	Operating principles are the choices DDx makes in response to the physics above.
172	
173	1. **Platform, not methodology** — DDx provides primitives; workflow tools provide opinions.
174	2. **Project-local by default** — install touches the project, not the machine.
175	3. **Artifacts are the product (documents primary, other media supported)** — code is output; artifacts are what you maintain.
176	4. **Bead-driven work** — beads are the unit of work; the queue is the interface.
177	5. **Cost-tiered throughput** — optimize closed beads per dollar, not raw capability.
178	6. **Git-native, file-first** — plain files, standard git, no lock-in.
179	7. **Agent-agnostic** — any capable harness plugs in via the prompt envelope.
180

100	  beads and agent sessions, project-scoped views for documents, dependency
101	  graph, and commit log, and layer-aware run-history routes against the unified
102	  substrate
103	
104	## Problem
105	
106	AI-assisted development needs more than prompt files. Teams need a shared way
107	to manage declarative artifacts, reusable runtime evidence, and local
108	automation infrastructure without hardcoding workflow semantics into each tool.
109	
110	The problems cluster by the physics they violate (see product-vision.md):
111	
112	**Abstraction** (Principle 1 — abstraction is the lever)
113	- **No structure**: Artifacts, prompts, personas, and patterns accumulate as
114	  ad hoc files with weak identity and no explicit relationships
115	- **No composition**: Assembling the right combination of persona + pattern +
116	  spec into agent context is manual and error-prone
117	- **No document integrity guarantees**: When an upstream document changes,
118	  dependent documents silently drift — no automatic staleness detection or
119	  reconciliation tasking
120	- **No transferability**: Framework knowledge is trapped in its author;
121	  onboarding new team members requires manual explanation
122	
123	**Iteration over tracked work** (Principle 2 — software is iteration over tracked work)
124	- **No reusable work-item store**: Workflow tools reimplement issue storage,
125	  dependency tracking, and coordination instead of sharing a local substrate
126	- **No reusable execution evidence**: Metrics, checks, and similar operations
127	  fall back to bespoke scripts and logs with no shared history model
128	
129	**Methodology plurality** (Principle 3 — methodology is plural)
130	- **No reusable agent dispatch**: Each tool grows its own harness registry,
131	  logging, and output-capture behavior — every workflow tool reinvents the same
132	  invocation plumbing
133	- **No reuse**: Every project reinvents its agent instructions and supporting
134	  mechanics from scratch; proven patterns stay trapped in individual repos
135	
136	**LLM physics** (Principle 4 — LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly)
137	- **No cost-tier enforcement**: Token cost is a first-order constraint, not an
138	  optimization. Without capability-keyed routing and model-selection guidance,
139	  teams overspend on routine work and have no signal on the cheapest model that
140	  reliably closes beads
141	
142	**Evidence and provenance** (Principle 5 — evidence provides memory)
143	- **No provenance for generated artifacts**: Generated files carry no record of
144	  which agent run, model, or prompt produced them — regeneration is ad hoc and
145	  lineage is lost
146	- **No measurement**: No standard way to track bead lifecycle metrics, token
147	  costs, or plugin-defined measures across projects
148	- **No feedback capture**: Lessons learned from agent interactions, project
149	  completions, and bead lifecycle stay informal — no structured way to capture,
150	  query, or act on what worked and what didn't
151	
152	**Human-AI collaboration** (Principle 6 — human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum)
153	- **No composition for handoffs**: Assembling the right artifact context for a
154	  human-to-agent or agent-to-human handoff is manual and ad hoc — no DDx
155	  primitive covers that assembly
156	- **No discoverability**: Developers can't easily browse what documents,
157	  artifacts, or local runtime evidence are available
158	- **No network access**: Agents and tools can only read state from the local
159	  filesystem unless projects build their own HTTP or MCP layer
160	
161	## Goals
162	
163	### Primary
164	1. **Manage multi-media artifact libraries** — provide structure, conventions,
165	   and CLI tooling so declarative project knowledge — documents, diagrams,
166	   wireframes, images, prompts, and other media — stays organized and
167	   agent-discoverable
168	2. **Provide reusable local runtime services** — expose beads, agent dispatch,
169	   and execution history as workflow-agnostic DDx primitives
170	3. **Enable document composition** — combine personas, patterns, specs, and
171	   templates into coherent agent context
172	4. **Serve project state to agents and tools** — expose documents, artifacts,
173	   beads, and execution evidence via MCP endpoints and HTTP
174	5. **Support cross-project reuse** — share document libraries and workflow
175	   plugins through an online registry (`ddx install`)
176	6. **Provide agent-facing skills for DDx operations** — ship interactive
177	   skills (slash commands) that guide agents through complex DDx CLI
178	   operations like bead triage, agent dispatch, and package installation
179	7. **Integrate with revision control** — auto-commit document changes to
180	   protect work, expose document history to agents and tools, enable
181	   write-then-commit workflows for MCP and UI clients
182	8. **Support multi-agent coordination** — make bead operations, document
183	   writes, and agent dispatch safe under concurrent multi-agent use, with
184	   MCP as the remote observation and control surface
185	9. **Embed essential utilities** — bundle common developer tools (jq, etc.)
186	   so workflow tools have a consistent, cross-platform base without external
187	   runtime dependencies
188	10. **Maintain artifact graph integrity** — track relationships between
189	    documents, detect staleness when upstream artifacts change, and generate
190	    reconciliation tasks automatically (FEAT-007)
191	11. **Track process metrics** — derive bead lifecycle cost, rework rates, and
192	    other process measures from existing stores (beads, agent sessions) so
193	    teams can understand the economics and efficiency of their workflow
194	    (FEAT-016)
195	12. **Stabilize the plugin API** — document existing extension surfaces, add
196	    schema versioning, and commit to backward compatibility so plugin authors
197	    can build with confidence (FEAT-018)
198	13. **Provide a three-layer run architecture** — ship `ddx run` (single agent
199	    invocation), `ddx try` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), and `ddx work`
200	    (mechanical queue drain) as DDx-owned primitives on one unified on-disk
201	    substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records (FEAT-010)
202	14. **Enable source-hash-driven regeneration of generated artifacts** — track
203	    which agent run, model, and prompt produced each generated artifact;
204	    support on-demand regeneration when the source changes (FEAT-005, FEAT-007)
205	15. **Expose 100% of DDx state via HTTP and MCP read endpoints** — every
206	    piece of CLI-visible DDx state (artifacts, beads, run history, graph,
207	    sidecar metadata) is readable over the network; write surfaces are added
208	    case-by-case as workflows demand, starting with `artifactRegenerate`
209	    (FEAT-002, FEAT-010)
210	
211	### Secondary
212	1. **Promote the practice** — website explains document-driven development and
213	   drives adoption
214	2. **Keep artifacts honest** — detect drift between governing documents and
215	   lower-level artifacts or runtime evidence
216	3. **Enable team transferability** — self-documenting project structures,
217	   getting-started guides, and pairing workflows so DDx is productive without
218	   requiring its author in the room
219	
220	### Success Metrics
221	
222	| Metric | Target |
223	|--------|--------|
224	| Time to assemble agent context | <30 seconds |
225	| Document reuse rate across projects | >40% |
226	| MCP endpoint response time | <200ms |
227	| Website explains DDx clearly to new visitor | <2 minutes to understand |
228	
229	### Non-Goals
230	
231	- A workflow methodology (that's HELIX and others, not DDx)
232	- Workflow-specific artifact ladders or stage progression (for example,
233	  `FEAT -> SD -> TD -> TP`) beyond storing IDs and relationships
234	- Workflow-specific bead validation (phase labels, spec-id enforcement — that's
235	  the workflow layer via hooks)
236	- Supervisory loop orchestration — DDx owns mechanical queue drain (`ddx
237	  work`); content-aware supervisory decisions (deciding what to do next based
238	  on agent or execution results — for example, "comparison failed → enqueue
239	  reconciliation beads") remain plugin/HELIX territory
240	- Cataloging run types beyond the three layers — comparison, replay,
241	  benchmark, and similar workflow shapes are skill compositions; DDx does not
242	  enshrine them in Go core or specs
243	- An AI agent or agent framework
244	- A standalone desktop GUI for editing documents (the embedded web UI editor
245	  in `ddx-server` is in-scope per FEAT-008; a separate desktop application is not)
246	- A cloud/SaaS service
247	- Real-time collaboration
248	- A storage system — artifacts are versioned in Git; future backends are
249	  possible but not DDx's concern
250	- Defining artifact types or templates — those come from plugins. DDx provides
251	  the infrastructure for storing and relating them.
252	- Operational metric definitions — plugins define what to measure; DDx
253	  provides the execution and collection infrastructure (FEAT-010)
254	- Optimization loop logic — DDx provides primitives (run, measure, compare);
255	  plugins define what to try next and when to converge
256	- Feedback collection features — beads already capture structured feedback;
257	  no separate feedback subsystem needed
258	
259	## Users

input.content:
---
title: Why DDx
description: "The new reality of agent-driven development, the pain it creates, the physics behind it, and the four primitives DDx provides in response."
weight: 1
---

# Why DDx

Agents now write most of the code. The tools, conventions, and habits we built for human-only teams quietly stop working when the team is half humans and half AI. DDx exists because the substrate underneath agent-driven development is missing — and without it, shipping software with agents degrades into prompt-and-pray.

This page lays out the argument: what changed, what hurts, why it hurts, and what we built in response.

## 1. The New Reality

A few things are true now that weren't true two years ago:

- **Agents execute, humans direct.** A working developer steers, reviews, and approves; the agent writes, edits, and runs the commands. The ratio of agent-authored to human-authored lines keeps climbing.
- **Every invocation starts from zero.** Agents have no persistent memory between runs. Whatever context an agent needs to do good work has to be assembled and handed in every time.
- **Output volume outpaces review capacity.** A single developer can dispatch more work in an hour than they could write in a week. The bottleneck moves from typing to deciding what to keep.
- **Cost is now a first-order constraint.** Token spend is real money on every iteration. Choosing the right model for the job is a daily concern, not a one-time configuration.
- **Work is parallel and interleaved.** Multiple agents drain a queue concurrently; humans pick up some beads, agents pick up others. Coordination has to survive concurrency.

The shape of "writing software" has changed. The tools haven't.

## 2. Six Pain Points

What teams hit, in the order they hit them:

### Constant context re-explanation
Every agent invocation requires re-stating the same project context: what we're building, the conventions, the prior decisions, the specs that govern this change. Without a shared, durable artifact substrate, this overhead repeats forever. Each session starts from zero.

### Specs and code drift apart
A spec is updated; the code isn't. Or the code changes; the spec doesn't. Without explicit relationships between artifacts and a way to detect staleness, drift accumulates silently until a major bug or onboarding session exposes it.

### Work-tracking reinvented per tool
Every workflow tool, every methodology, every project ends up rebuilding the same issue store, dependency tracker, and ready-queue from scratch. The work substrate is local infrastructure that should be shared, not re-implemented per tool.

### Execution evidence evaporates
Agents run, produce output, and that output disappears into a terminal scrollback. There's no durable record of what was run, what model was used, what tokens were spent, what files changed, or what the result was. Without evidence, evaluation, debugging, and learning all degrade to anecdote.

### Routine work runs on premium models
Without capability-aware routing, every bead runs on the same model — usually the strongest one configured. Cheap, reliable, mechanical work pays the price of frontier-model inference. There's no signal on which model is the cheapest one that reliably closes which kind of bead.

### Agents re-learn each project's CLI
Every project has a slightly different shape. Without a discoverable, consistent surface, agents waste tokens probing for commands, conventions, and entry points. The harness keeps re-deriving facts the project already knows.

## 3. The Root Cause

Step back from the symptoms and the underlying issue is one sentence:

> **Agents execute in isolation with no shared memory.**

Every pain above is downstream of this. Context re-explanation is the absence of shared memory across sessions. Spec/code drift is the absence of shared memory across artifacts. Work-tracking reinvention is the absence of shared memory across tools. Evidence loss is the absence of shared memory across runs. Cost overspend is the absence of shared memory about what worked at what tier. Project re-learning is the absence of shared memory about the project's own surface.

Patching symptom-by-symptom doesn't fix it. What's needed is a substrate that gives agent work a place to stand — durable, file-based, agent-discoverable, and shared across every invocation.

DDx answers with four primitives:

1. **Artifacts** — versioned, related, discoverable project knowledge. Documents, diagrams, prompts, personas, and other media live in the repo with explicit identity and relationships. The artifact graph tracks staleness when an upstream artifact changes.
2. **Beads** — the unit of work. Self-contained items with acceptance criteria and a dependency DAG. The ready/blocked queue is the interface between intent and execution. Beads make intent legible to humans and agents alike.
3. **Tracked execution (`ddx work`)** — drain the bead queue. Each attempt runs in an isolated worktree, captures evidence (model, tokens, files changed, exit metadata), and either merges back on success or preserves the result for inspection. The history persists.
4. **Skills** — a single, consolidated agent-facing surface. One DDx skill teaches an agent how to operate the toolkit: bead triage, agent dispatch, plugin installation, evidence inspection. Agents stop probing; they read the skill once.

These four primitives are the shared memory the agent-driven workflow was missing.

## 4. The Physics

Underneath the pain points and the response are six load-bearing claims about how software gets built and how generative AI behaves. We call these the *physics* — facts you can either respect or pay the cost of ignoring.

### Physics of Software

1. **Abstraction is the lever.** Multi-level artifact stacks with maintained relationships are how intent propagates without being lost. True for human teams; load-bearing for agents because they don't carry tacit knowledge between invocations. If the artifact stack isn't there, every invocation rebuilds context from scratch and gets it slightly wrong.

2. **Software is iteration over tracked work.** Repeated trials over an explicit work substrate — beads, queues, dependency DAGs — is how software gets built. This pre-existed agents; agents make the substrate non-optional. Untracked work cannot be drained, evaluated, or coordinated across multiple agents.

3. **Methodology is plural.** Different teams, projects, and problem domains demand different workflows — waterfall, agile, kanban, ad-hoc. No tool that bakes one in survives the rest. The platform must provide primitives that any methodology composes; opinions belong in optional plugins.

### Physics of Generative AI

4. **LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly.** Quality degrades as the context window fills. Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and "cheapest model that reliably closes the bead" are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations to add later. Treating LLM calls as if they were deterministic function calls produces brittle workflows and surprise bills.

5. **Evidence provides memory.** Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what was captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.

### The Intersection

6. **Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum.** Abstraction levers intent across the artifact stack, but only collaboration converts leverage into shipped software. Humans supply intent and accountability; AI supplies volume and execution. Handoffs run in both directions, at every level of the stack — and the toolkit at the seam decides whether those handoffs are smooth or expensive.

The four primitives are the choices DDx makes in response to these six. Artifacts honor #1. Beads honor #2 and #3. Tracked execution honors #4 and #5. Skills honor #6.

## 5. DDx as the Response

Put it together and the operating model is straightforward:

- **Project knowledge lives in the repo as artifacts.** Plain files, plain git, agent-discoverable. Identity and relationships are explicit, not inferred. Staleness is detected, not discovered the hard way.
- **Work flows through beads.** Anything an agent does is against a bead. The bead carries acceptance criteria; review checks the criteria, not memory of the conversation. The queue is the contract between intent and execution.
- **Agents run via `ddx work`.** The queue drains in isolated worktrees, evidence is captured automatically, and successful results merge back. Failed or timed-out runs are preserved for inspection. Token cost, model choice, and outcome are all recorded.
- **Agents learn the toolkit through one skill.** One consolidated DDx skill replaces a fleet of project-specific instructions. Every project that uses DDx looks the same to the agent.
- **Everything is local-first.** `ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only touch the project root. There's no home-directory state, no machine-wide opinions, no proprietary backend. Cloning the repo gives you the whole picture.
- **Every primitive is workflow-agnostic.** DDx ships no methodology. Phases, gates, and supervisory loops belong in plugins, not in the platform.

The result: agents stop starting from zero. Work has a place to live. Evidence persists. The substrate that agent-driven development was missing is on disk, in the repo, in plain files — exactly where everything else already is.

That's why DDx exists.

---

Ready to try it? Start with [Get Started](/docs/getting-started/) or browse the [Features](/features/).
